questions.question: Explain the system boot process with a neat diagram.
keyPoints: Power on triggers CPU to execute firmware (BIOS/UEFI) from ROM
POST (Power-On Self-Test) checks hardware integrity
Firmware reads MBR/GPT to locate bootloader
Bootloader (GRUB) loaded and displays boot menu
Bootloader loads kernel (vmlinuz) and initrd into memory
Kernel initializes hardware, mounts root filesystem
Kernel starts init/systemd (PID 1)
System services started, login screen displayed
Draw the complete boot sequence diagram

questions.question: Compare BIOS and UEFI.
keyPoints: BIOS: text-based, uses MBR, 2 TB disk limit, 4 primary partitions, 16-bit mode
UEFI: graphical, uses GPT, no disk size limit, 128 partitions, 32/64-bit mode
UEFI supports Secure Boot, BIOS does not
UEFI has faster boot times
UEFI stores boot programs in EFI System Partition

questions.question: What is a bootloader? Explain the role of GRUB in the boot process.
keyPoints: Bootloader is a program that loads the OS kernel from disk into memory
GRUB is the most common Linux bootloader
Stage 1 in MBR loads Stage 2
Stage 2 displays boot menu for kernel selection
GRUB loads selected kernel and initrd into memory
Passes boot parameters (kernel command line) to the kernel

questions.question: Differentiate between MBR and GPT partition schemes.
keyPoints: MBR: 512 bytes, max 2 TB disk, max 4 primary partitions, used with BIOS
GPT: no practical size limit, up to 128 partitions, used with UEFI
GPT has backup partition table at end of disk
GPT uses CRC32 checksums for integrity
GPT partitions identified by unique GUIDs
